vulnerability.title: Verify Permissions on cron.weekly
vulnerability.description: To properly set the permissions of /etc/cron.weekly, run the command: $ sudo chmod 0700 /etc/cron.weekly
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # Remediation is applicable only in certain platforms if rpm --quiet -q kernel-core; then find -H /etc/cron.weekly/ -maxdepth 0 -perm /u+s,g+xwrs,o+xwrt -type d -exec chmod u-s,g-xwrs,o-xwrt {} \; else >&2 echo 'Remediation is not applicable, nothing was done' fi

triage_decision.reason: The remediation involves setting strict permissions (0700) on /etc/cron.weekly and removing special bits (setuid, setgid, sticky). This directory contains system cron jobs executed by root. Changing permissions to restrict access to root only is a standard hardening measure with minimal risk. It does not affect boot, authentication, networking, or filesystem structure. The worst-case impact is that non-root users lose access to view or modify these cron jobs, which is intentional and expected.
triage_decision.estimated_impact: If custom cron jobs rely on non-root users having read/execute access to /etc/cron.weekly (highly unusual and against best practices), those jobs may fail to run. However, system cron directories should be root-owned and restricted per security guidelines.; Incorrect execution of the remediation command (e.g., typos) could affect other paths, but the provided script is scoped to the specific directory.
